title: Hitler's Rise to Power, 1919–1933
summary: Hitler's rise from obscure political agitator to Chancellor of Germany in fourteen years is one of history's most studied examples of democratic collapse.

sections.heading: Munich, 1919–1923
sections.text: Hitler joined the tiny DAP in September 1919 and rapidly took control, transforming it into the NSDAP. His oratorical gifts drew thousands to his speeches. The failed Beer Hall Putsch of November 1923 resulted in his trial and imprisonment, during which he dictated Mein Kampf — turning a tactical defeat into a propaganda victory.

sections.heading: The Legal Path, 1925–1929
sections.text: Released from prison in December 1924, Hitler rebuilt the NSDAP and committed to achieving power through elections rather than revolution. The party remained a fringe movement through the late 1920s, winning only 2.6% of the vote in May 1928.

sections.heading: Depression and Breakthrough, 1929–1932
sections.text: The Great Depression transformed the political landscape. Unemployment reaching 6 million in 1932 destroyed faith in the Weimar system. NSDAP support surged: 18.3% in September 1930, 37.4% in July 1932 — making it Germany's largest party. Goebbels's propaganda machine, mass rallies, and the SA's intimidation tactics combined with genuine popular appeal.

sections.heading: Appointment as Chancellor, 30 January 1933
sections.text: NSDAP support actually fell slightly to 33.1% in November 1932. Franz von Papen convinced President Hindenburg that appointing Hitler Chancellor in a conservative cabinet would allow them to control him. Hitler was appointed Chancellor on 30 January 1933. Within months, the Reichstag Fire, the Enabling Act, and the elimination of all competing parties had transformed Germany into a dictatorship.
